answers.intake_q1: When did your elbow pain start?
answers.intake_q2: Can you describe what caused your elbow pain or any injury related to it?
answers.intake_a2: I don't know, it just started hurting while I was playing tennis
answers.intake_q3: Can you describe the type of pain and if it worsens with any specific movements or activities?
answers.intake_a3: it is sharp and worse with playing tennis
answers.intake_q4: Any swelling, redness, or warmth around the elbow?
answers.intake_q5: Any numbness, tingling, or weakness in your arm or hand?
answers.intake_q6: Any difficulty moving your elbow or decreased range of motion?
answers.intake_q7: Have you tried any treatments or medications for your elbow pain so far?
answers.intake_a7: I iced it on the first day
answers.intake_q8: Any fever, chills, or other systemic symptoms since the elbow pain started?
answers.intake_q9: Have you noticed any tenderness when pressing on the outside of your elbow?
answers.intake_q10: Have you noticed any pain or tenderness on the inside of your elbow, or only on the outside?

note.soap:
S: 
The patient is a 45-year-old female presenting with a 3-week history of sharp pain localized to the outside of her elbow. The pain began insidiously while playing tennis and worsens with tennis activity. She reports tenderness on the outside of the elbow but denies any swelling, redness, warmth, numbness, tingling, weakness, or decreased range of motion. She has no systemic symptoms such as fever or chills. Initial self-treatment included icing on the first day. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
Phone evaluation only; no physical exam or vital signs available.

A: 
The clinical presentation is consistent with lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow), characterized by lateral elbow pain exacerbated by activity and localized tenderness. This is a common overuse injury in tennis players. 

Differential: 
1. Lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow) 
2. Radial tunnel syndrome 
3. Osteoarthritis of the elbow 
4. Referred pain from cervical radiculopathy 

P: 
- First-line medication: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen or naproxen for pain relief and inflammation reduction. 
- Alternatives: Acetaminophen if NSAIDs are contraindicated or not tolerated. 
- Medication safety considerations: Use NSAIDs with caution in patients with gastrointestinal, renal, or cardiovascular risk factors; ensure dosing does not exceed recommended limits. Acetaminophen should be used within safe daily limits to avoid hepatotoxicity. 
- Return precautions: Advise the patient to seek care if pain worsens, if there is new onset of numbness, weakness, swelling, redness, or warmth around the elbow, or if systemic symptoms such as fever develop. 
- Testing: Not indicated at this time based on clinical presentation; consider imaging or specialist referral if symptoms persist beyond 6-8 weeks or worsen despite conservative management.

evidence.title: Tennis elbow - NHS
evidence.snippet: rvices without needing a referral from a GP. Get NHS help for back or joint problems Treatments for tennis elbow Tennis elbow usually gets better after resting your arm for a few weeks, but it can sometimes last longer. A GP may give you anti-inflammatory tablets. They may also refer you for physiotherapy if your symptoms have not improved after trying treat…

evidence.title: Elbow Injuries | Elbow Disorders | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: bone, cartilage, ligaments and fluid. Muscles and tendons help the elbow joint move. When any of these structures is hurt or diseased, you have elbow problems. Many things can make your elbow hurt. A common cause is tendinitis , an inflammation or injury to the tendons that attach muscle to bone. Tendinitis of the elbow is a sports injury , often from playin…

evidence.title: Rheumatoid Arthritis | RA | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: joints. RA affects people differently. You might have the disease for only a short time, or your symptoms might come and go. The severe form can be chronic (long-lasting) and may last a lifetime. Symptoms of rheumatoid arthritis may include: Joint pain or stiffness when moving. This is usually worse in the morning or after inactivity. RA usually affects both…
